About this Dataset

This dataset tracks the estimated prevalence of five major mental health disorders: Depressive Disorders, Anxiety Disorders, Bipolar Disorders, Schizophrenia, and Eating Disorders, across 195 countries from 1990 to 2019, sourced from the Global Burden of Disease (GBD) Study. Prevalence figures represent the estimated share of each country’s population affected in a given year. Across the study period, Anxiety Disorders is the most prevalent disorder globally (avg. 4.12%), while Eating Disorders is the least common.

The relationship between national wealth and mental health rates is real but nuanced. Countries above the global GDP median average a combined disorder prevalence of 9.3%, compared to 8.71% for lower-income nations, a modest gap of roughly 0.59 percentage points. This reflects a well-documented paradox: wealthier nations tend to report higher rates partly because they have greater diagnostic infrastructure and cultural acceptance of mental health disclosure, not necessarily because their populations are less mentally healthy. The effect is especially pronounced for Eating Disorders (Pearson r = 0.355 with log GDP), which is strongly associated with high-income, Western countries. By contrast, Depressive Disorders shows almost no correlation with GDP (r = 0.02), suggesting its prevalence is distributed more evenly across income levels worldwide.

Geographically, the Middle East & North Africa region carries the highest average total burden at 10.36%, while East Asia & Pacific reports the lowest at 7.61%. At the country level, Portugal records the highest combined prevalence across all five disorders at 14.25%, nearly double the global average, largely driven by elevated Anxiety and Depressive Disorder rates.

Use the GDP vs. Mental Health page to explore correlation patterns and regional breakdowns interactively, or the Mental Health Overview page for country-level rankings and a global prevalence map.
